Salk Institute · Laboratory of Genetics

Neuronal Plasticity
and Genomic Diversity

We study how the adult brain changes itself. How new neurons appear in places they were once thought not to grow, how aging rewrites the cells inside us, and what all of that means for diseases we have not yet solved.

Explore Discoveries Join the Lab
0Years at Salk
0Publications
0Major Awards
0Lab Members
"The brain retains a remarkable capacity for change, and that changes everything we thought we knew about neurological disease."
Principal Investigator

Fred H. "Rusty" Gage

Professor · Laboratory of Genetics

Vi and John Adler Chair for Research on Age-Related Neurodegenerative Disease

For most of the twentieth century, neuroscience taught that you were born with all the neurons you would ever have. In 1998, Rusty and his collaborators showed that was wrong. The adult human brain grows new neurons throughout life. That finding rewrote textbooks and reshaped the questions our lab still asks today.

The work has never sat in one place. From environmental enrichment in mice, to LINE-1 retrotransposons jumping around the genomes of individual neurons, to turning a patient's own skin cells into the neurons they lost, the connecting thread is curiosity about how brains stay flexible. And about how, eventually, they don't.

Rusty served as President of the Salk Institute from 2018 to 2023. He is a member of the National Academy of Sciences, the National Academy of Medicine, and the American Academy of Arts and Sciences. His research continues to focus on fundamental questions about neuronal plasticity, brain aging, and the cellular basis of neurological disease.

Google Scholar Salk Profile
Adult Neurogenesis Cellular Reprogramming Alzheimer's Disease iPSC Modeling LINE-1 Retrotransposons Neuronal Mosaicism Human Evolution
Scientific Legacy

Four Decades of Discovery

Click any card to see the science behind the discovery. Each milestone is grounded in published research from the Gage Laboratory.

1998

Neurogenesis in the Adult Human Brain

The textbooks said adult humans do not make new neurons. We found them in postmortem hippocampal tissue from cancer patients who had received BrdU before death. The dogma was wrong. People can grow new brain cells for as long as they live.

Eriksson, Gage et al. · Nature Medicine ↗

Click to see figure

BrdU-positive newborn neurons co-labeled with NeuN in the adult human dentate gyrus, the original direct evidence of human neurogenesis.

View paper
2000

Mammalian Neural Stem Cells

A single-author review in Science that pulled the field together. What is a neural stem cell? Where do they live? What can they become? The answers were still forming, and this paper helped consolidate them.

Gage · Science ↗

Click to see figure

Differentiation potential of neural stem cells into multiple neuronal phenotypes, identified by catecholamine pathway markers (DDC, DBH, PNMT) and prohormone convertases.

View paper
2005

Exercise Enhances Neurogenesis in Aged Mice

Old mice that ran on a wheel grew more new neurons in the hippocampus than sedentary old mice. They also learned better. A clean result with serious implications for human aging and cognitive decline.

van Praag, Gage et al. · J Neurosci ↗

Click to see figure

Perimeter and surface area of new vasculature increased with running (YR), and the dentate gyrus showed prominent blood vessel remodeling.

View paper
2009

Environment Modulates LINE-1 Retrotransposons

Mobile elements are not just spectators. In the adult hippocampus, voluntary exercise increased LINE-1 retrotransposition events in neurons. The outside world reaches into the genome itself.

Muotri, Gage et al. · Hippocampus ↗

Click to see figure

EGFP-positive neurons marking active LINE-1 retrotransposition events in the adult hippocampus, modulated by environmental enrichment.

View paper
2015

Adult Neurogenesis Across Species

A review pulling together comparative work on adult neurogenesis across mammals. How conserved is the process? Where does it happen? What does that tell us about its function in humans?

Gage et al. · Current Opinion Behav Sci ↗

Click to see figure

Environmental enrichment drives adult hippocampal neurogenesis, which supports contextual discrimination in memory tasks.

View paper
2015

Direct Conversion Preserves Aging Signatures

iPSCs are great, but the reprogramming step wipes the cell's age. So if you want to model an old person's brain, iPSC-derived neurons lie to you. We showed that direct conversion of fibroblasts to neurons keeps the aging signature intact, identifying RanBP17 as a key aging-related factor.

Mertens, Gage et al. ↗

Click to see figure

Aging donor fibroblasts converted directly to induced neurons retain age signatures, while iPSC reprogramming rejuvenates the cells.

View paper
2018

Patient-Derived Neurons Reveal Schizophrenia Phenotype

Induced neurons from people with schizophrenia showed an unexpected enrichment of dopaminergic markers compared with controls. Direct conversion let us see a cellular signature of the disease that other methods missed.

View on PubMed Central ↗

Click to see figure

TH-positive neurons enriched in schizophrenia patient-derived induced neurons compared to controls.

View paper
2022

Senescent Neurons Drive Inflammation in Alzheimer's

Neurons in Alzheimer's brains do something strange. They become senescent, like dividing cells in an aging body, except neurons do not divide. They sit there, alive but broken, leaking inflammatory signals into the tissue. Clearing them with senolytics helped neighbors recover.

Herdy, Gage et al. · Cell Stem Cell ↗

Click to see figure

Post-mitotic neurons enter senescence in Alzheimer's, triggering reactive astrogliosis via SASP. Senolytics eliminate the senescent cells.

View paper
2024

An Astrocyte-Rich Brain Organoid

Half the brain is glia, but most lab models barely have any. We built a glia-enriched cortical organoid that grows mature, functional astrocytes, then transplanted it into mouse brain. Now we can finally watch glia and neurons interact in something close to a real environment.

Wang, Gage et al. · Nat Biotechnology ↗

Click to see figure

Human astrocyte morphology and maturation in the glia-enriched organoid, with diverse subtypes from interlaminar to fibrous.

View paper
2025

Selective CK2A2 Inhibitors for CNS Activity

Going after a brain-enriched kinase paralog. Tucker, Da Silva, and Gage used scaffold hopping from a flavone starting point to discover selective CK2A2 inhibitors with the properties needed to actually reach the brain.

Tucker, Da Silva, Gage · Bioorg Med Chem ↗

Click to see figure

Scaffold hopping from the flavone FMN-79 yielded brain-penetrant CK2A2 inhibitors with strong selectivity over CK2A1.

View paper
2026

APOE4 Impairs Lipoprotein Secretion in Microglia

The biggest genetic risk factor for late-onset Alzheimer's is APOE4. Revanna and the lab show that microglia carrying APOE4 fail to secrete lipoproteins properly. Cholesterol and lipids pile up inside, lysosomes choke, and mitochondria break. A direct line from genotype to cellular failure.

Revanna, Jones, Gage et al. · bioRxiv ↗

Click to see figure

APOE4 microglia accumulate intracellular lipids, dysregulate lysosomes, and trigger mitochondrial dysfunction and ROS.

View paper
What We Work On

Research Areas

Four lines of work, all knotted together. You cannot really understand any of them in isolation.

01
🧠

Neurogenesis

New neurons keep showing up in the adult dentate gyrus, decades after development is supposed to be done. We want to know what they do. Live imaging in awake animals plus single-cell sequencing during learning is starting to give us answers about how memory actually gets written.

HippocampusMemoryLive ImagingSingle-Cell
02
🔬

Disease in a Dish

Skin cells from a patient become stem cells, then become the neurons that patient lost. With iPSCs and direct conversion, we model schizophrenia, autism, bipolar disorder, Parkinson's, MS, and Alzheimer's, and we look for the cellular phenotype that explains the clinical one.

iPSCDirect ConversionParkinson'sSchizophrenia
03
🔀

Neuronal Mosaicism

LINE-1 and Alu retrotransposons jump around the genome during neurogenesis. The neuron sitting next to another neuron in your hippocampus is not quite the same as it genetically. Single-cell sequencing lets us catch these events, and we are trying to figure out when they matter for disease.

LINE-1AluSomatic MosaicismNGS
04
🦍

Human Evolution

We make iPSCs from chimpanzees, bonobos, and gorillas, then grow neurons from them alongside human ones. What is actually different about us at the cellular level? It is a question you cannot answer with fossils, only with living cells.

Non-Human PrimatesiPSCEvolution

People

The Team

The lab is the people. Some have been here for years, some are passing through. All of it works because of them.

Fred H. "Rusty" Gage

Professor and Principal Investigator

Vi and John Adler Chair for Research on Age-Related Neurodegenerative Disease

Laboratory of Genetics. National Academy of Sciences. National Academy of Medicine. American Academy of Arts and Sciences. President of Salk Institute 2018 to 2023. Past President, Society for Neuroscience and ISSCR. PhD, Johns Hopkins.

Innovation

Patents

Three representative patents from Rusty's body of inventions, spanning thirty years of work on viral vectors, stem cell reprogramming, and Alzheimer's diagnostics. Click any card to read the patent.

💉
US5919667A · 1999

Modular Assembly Retroviral Vectors and Uses Thereof

Retroviral vectors with modified long terminal repeats that maintain high-level, ligand-modulatable gene expression even in growth-arrested primary cells, where standard retroviral vectors fail.

View on Google Patents ↗
🧬
US9340775B2 · 2016

Footprint-Free Induced Pluripotent Stem Cells from Human Neural Stem Cells

Methods for generating iPSCs by transfecting human neural stem cells with an episomal vector encoding Oct4 and Nanog, avoiding genomic integration of the reprogramming factors.

View on Google Patents ↗
🔬
US20250122570A1 · 2025

Epigenome Biomarkers for Identifying Alzheimer's Disease

DNA methylation signatures across multiple loci used to identify Alzheimer's disease in cells or patients, and to screen candidate therapeutics by tracking how they change those signatures.

View on Google Patents ↗
See All Patents on Google

News and Press

From the Lab and the Institute

A selection worth reading.

2026

Eight Salk Scientists Among the World's Most Highly Cited

Rusty was named to Clarivate's 2025 Highly Cited Researchers list alongside seven other Salk colleagues. The list recognizes scientists whose papers rank in the top one percent for citations over the past decade.

Read on Inside Salk ↗

Image: inside.salk.edu

November 2024

Ogawa-Yamanaka Stem Cell Prize

Awarded by Gladstone Institutes and Cell Press. Past recipients include Shinya Yamanaka. The prize recognizes scientists who have used stem cell technology to make paradigm-shifting contributions.

Read on Gladstone ↗

Image: Gladstone Institutes

August 2024

Taylor International Prize in Medicine

One of Canada's most prestigious medical research awards, recognizing transformative work in aging neuroscience. The prize includes a $50,000 award and a public lecture at Western University.

Read on Salk.edu ↗

Image: Schulich Medicine, Western University

February 2024

A Brain Organoid With Real Astrocytes

Published in Nature Biotechnology. The lab built a glia-enriched cortical organoid that, when transplanted into mouse brain, develops the morphological diversity of real human astrocytes for the first time.

Read on Salk.edu ↗

Image: Salk Institute

December 2022

Neuronal Senescence in Alzheimer's

Published in Cell Stem Cell. The lab found that neurons in Alzheimer's patients enter a senescent, zombie-like state and leak inflammatory signals into surrounding tissue. Clearing them in a dish helped neighbors recover.

Read on Salk.edu ↗

Image: Salk Institute

July 2016

Unlocking Secrets of Autism with Stem Cells

CIRM covered the lab's work using patient-derived iPSCs to model autism in a dish. Neurons from people with autism showed faster precursor cell division and altered bursting activity, partially rescued by IGF-1.

Read on CIRM Blog ↗

Image: CIRM

Open Positions

We are recruiting at every career stage. You should be curious, rigorous, and comfortable with the fact that experiments mostly fail before they work. Rotation students are very welcome.
